coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of tunable fluorescent carbon dots and their third-order nonlinear optical properties
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2016 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Li
Xiang, Weidong
Gao, Haihong
Wang, Jiangyan
Ni, Yingying
Liang, Xiaojuan
coredata.dc:description: We report the synthesis and luminescent properties of carbon dots whose peak fluorescence emission are independent of excitation wavelength. Meanwhile, varying the concentrations of carbon dots can achieve a shift in the emission wavelength. Detailed structural characterization and optical properties of carbon dots with different concentrations were studied. Particularly, the blue-shifted excitation-independent emission peaks were observed when the concentration of carbon dots decreased. The decreased fluorescent properties were considered to originate from the surface modification of carbon dots. Additionally, we investigated the third-order nonlinear optical responses of carbon dots with different concentrations by Z-scan technique with single 190 fs laser pulses at 532 nm. It was found that both samples exhibited positive nonlinear refractivity and negative nonlinear absorption coefficient in the fs regime. These tunable optical properties would provide more possibilities for the utility of carbon dots in optical devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Tunable fluorescence
Nonlinear optical properties
Z-scan technique
Stuctrual characterization
Excitation-independent emission

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Third-order non-linear optical properties of CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FTIR spectra of CDs with different concentrations.
The TEM image (a) Insets is the diameter distribution for over 50 particles, (b) HRTEM image of prepared CDs. (c) High-resolution XPS full spectra of CDs. (d) C 1s level of typical CDs.
(a) UV–vis absorption spectra and (b) photographs of CDs samples with different concentrations under a 365 nm UV lamp and visible light.

Excitation-independent PL emission spectra of CDs with different concentrations (a) C, (b) 1/2C, (c) 1/5C, (d) 1/10C and (e) 1/20C under different excitation wavelengths recorded from 360 nm to 480 nm in 20 nm increments. (e) PL emission peak of CDs with different concentrations.
Fluorescence decay curves for CDs with different concentrations.

The “open-aperture” Z-scan normalized transmittance curves of the CDs with different concentrations under 532 nm, 190 fs laser excitation and the single-pulse energy of 0.07 μJ: (a) C; (b) 1/2C; (c) 1/5C; (d) 1/10C; (e) 1/20C; (f) C2H5OH. (Theoretical data in hollow circular and fitting curves in solid line).

The “close-aperture” Z-scan normalized transmittance curves of the CDs with different concentrations under 532 nm, 190 fs laser excitation and the single-pulse energy of 0.07 μJ (Theoretical data in hollow circular and fitting curves in solid line).
Biexponential fit values of CDs with different concentrations.
The Third-order nonlinear optical parameters of CDs with different concentrations.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of tunable fluorescent carbon dots and their third-order nonlinear optical properties

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials Engineering, Wenzhou University, Wenzhou 325035, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Chemistry and Materials Engineering, Wenzhou University, Wenzhou, Zhejiang, 325035, China. Tel.: +86 577 86596013; fax: +86 577 86689644.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report the synthesis and luminescent properties of carbon dots whose peak fluorescence emission are independent of excitation wavelength. Meanwhile, varying the concentrations of carbon dots can achieve a shift in the emission wavelength. Detailed structural characterization and optical properties of carbon dots with different concentrations were studied. Particularly, the blue-shifted excitation-independent emission peaks were observed when the concentration of carbon dots decreased. The decreased fluorescent properties were considered to originate from the surface modification of carbon dots. Additionally, we investigated the third-order nonlinear optical responses of carbon dots with different concentrations by Z-scan technique with single 190 fs laser pulses at 532 nm. It was found that both samples exhibited positive nonlinear refractivity and negative nonlinear absorption coefficient in the fs regime. These tunable optical properties would provide more possibilities for the utility of carbon dots in optical devices.

ce:section.ce:para: Photoluminescent carbon dots (CDs) have received considerable attention due to their low-toxic, multicolor emission, controlled surface chemistry, good solvent dispersability, excellent photostability as well as superior optical properties [1,2]. Moreover, the surface of CDs can easily be functionalized, doping with nitrogen and sulfur or metal ions may further alter the optical properties of the CDs or even add new functionalities [3–5]. Obtaining tunable fluorescence emission has been recognized as a key requirement for successfully implementing CDs in nearly all practical applications [6,7]. Many studies have reported CDs with tunable photoluminescence (PL) by varying the CDs chemical and physical properties. Jana et al. synthesized highly fluorescent carbon nanoparticles (FCDs) by carbohydrate carbonization method for biological labeling and imaging, which have the particle sizes between 1 and 10 nm and produce tunable emissions such as blue, green, yellow and red [8]. Hu et al. reported the tunable excitation-dependent fluorescence CDs across the entire visible spectrum excited by white light [9]. Pang et al. prepared a series of CDs exhibiting various colors of excitation-independent fluorescence by varying their degree of surface oxidation and their size [10]. Although many reports have presented excitation-dependent fluorescence emission, typical significant decrease in fluorescence emission intensity [11–13], the excitation-independent fluorescence emission with shifts in the emission wavelength without compromising the peak emission intensity is rarely reported. Nonetheless, the properties of fluorescent CDs remain largely unexplored. So a significant amount of work is still highly needed in this area, including using facile method to achieve tunable PL emission wavelengths of CDs without compromising the peak emission intensity.

In recent years, in addition to the PL and electroluminescent properties of CDs have been thoroughly investigated, the non-linear optical (NLO) properties of CDs have received some attention. As previous reported, CDs exhibited NLO properties significantly dependent on surface passivation and the nature of the organic moieties [14,15]. B.Bourlinos groups for the first time studied the NLO optical properties of CDs and boron doped CDs under 4 ns and 35 ps, 532 nm and 1064 nm laser excitation conditions by Z-scan technique. They attempted to explore the underlying physical mechanisms between the intrinsic nonlinear absorption and refraction properties of CDs [16–19]. Qiu et al. investigated nonlinear optical response of as-prepared CNDs with 100 fs laser pulses at 800 nm by open-aperture Z-scan technique, the strong two photon absorption of CNDs were observed [20]. At present, the study about NLO optical properties of CDs are still at the early stages of development, much work are highly needed in this area.

In this study, we prepared a series of CDs whose presented tunable excitation-independent fluorescence by simple adjustment of the concentrations. The prepared silane pre-functionalized CDs solution by hot-inject method were diluted by ethanol providing stable, optically transparent dispersions with different colors. The structure of CDs with different concentrations were studied using Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR), transmission electron microscope (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). In particular, the blue-shifted excitation-independent emissions peaks from 620 to 530 nm accompanied by remarkable increase in fluorescence emission intensity were observed when the concentration of CDs decreased. The quantum efficiency and the PL decay times of the as-prepared CDs were also varied. Specifically, we revealed that the decreased fluorescent properties of the CDs were considered to originate from the surface modification of CDs. in addition, we investigated the third-order nonlinear optical responses of silane pre-functionalized CDs with different concentrations by Z-scan technique with single 190 fs laser pulses at 532 nm. It was found that both CDs exhibited positive nonlinear refractivity and negative nonlinear absorption coefficient in the fs regime. Both the nonlinear refractivity and negative nonlinear absorption coefficient were decreased when the concentration of CDs decreased.

Anhydrous citric acid, ethyl alcohol, N-(β-aminoethyl)-γ-aminopropyl methldimethoxy silane (AEAPMS) and all other chemicals were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All the chemicals were of analytical grade and were used as received without any further purification.

The CDs were prepared by modifying a previous reported method.21 Briefly, 20 mL AEAPMS were placed into a three-necked flask and were degassed with nitrogen for 10 min. When the temperature reached 200 °C, 1.5 g citric acid dissolved uniformly with 4 mL ethanol was quickly injected into the solution with vigorous stirring. The mixture was kept at the temperature for 1 min, during which the solution changed from a colorless liquid to yellow and then finally dark brown clustered solid, indicating the formation of SiCDs (CDs). Subsequently, the CDs solution was cooled to room temperature and diluted to different concentrations with ethanol. Finally, the different concentrations of CDs solution were obtained (original concentration C), which were labeled as C, 1/2C, 1/5C, 1/10C, 1/20C respectively for convenience.

Fluorescence spectra and PL QYs were recorded with a Horiba Jobin Yvon Fluromax-4P spectrophotometer equipped with absolute QY measurement apparatus. For the UV/vis absorption, the UV-2600 spectrometer was used at room temperature. Time-resolved PL lifetime measurements were carried out using a time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) lifetime spectroscopy system with a picosecond-pulsed diode laser (EPL-460 nm) as the single wavelength excitation light source. The TEM and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) measurements were carried out with a FEI Tecnai F20 performing with an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. In obtaining the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra, the sample was grounded with KBr power and was measured using Bruke Equinox 55 FTIR spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out using an Axi Ultra DLD spectrometer with monochrome Al Ka as the excitation source. The XRD patterns were obtained using an X-ray diffract meter (Bruker D8 Advance) in the 2θ range of 10–80° using Cu Kα radiation with wavelength λ of 1.5406 Å. Temperature dependent emission spectra were measured with a Horiba Jobin−Yvon Fluorolog-3 FL3-211 spectrometer equipped with a 450W xenon lamp as the excitation source. The optical absorption was obtained by a Perkin Elmer Lambda 750 UV–vis Spectrometer. All the measurements were carried out at room temperature.

The blue-Green, green, yellow-green, yellow, orange CDs with emissions at 530 nm, 550 nm, 575 nm, 605 nm, 620 nm respectively could be obtained by varying the concentrations from C to 1/20C, and were labeled as BG-CDs, G-CDs, YG-CDs, Y-CDs, O-CDs respectively for convenience. The relevant parameters for the various samples with different concentrations were summarized in Table S1 and are discussed in next section.

The surface groups were identified by FTIR and XPS. Fig. 1 shows the variation of surface groups of a series of CDs. With varying the concentrations of CDs, no new IR peak appears, but the relative intensities of different peaks changed obviously. Typical signals for –NH2 groups at 3450 cm−1 and the CO vibration at 1654 cm−1. In addition, CH2 bending (1450 cm−1), Si–O–Si stretching (1070–1098 cm−1), and Si–CH2 stretching (790 cm−1) bands appeared in all spectra. Remarkably, the peak intensity of the Si–O–Si stretching group increased with the decrease of concentration of CDs, which was due to further modification. The chemical composition of CDs was further analyzed by a series of XPS analyses. The full XPS spectrum revealed that CDs is mainly composed of carbon, oxygen, silicon, and nitrogen in descending order of content (Fig. 2 c). The C1s XPS spectra of CDs in Fig. 2d showed that the following contribution can be assigned: CC (284.3 eV), C–O (285.7 eV) and Si–CH2 (282 eV), which was well-consistent with FTIR spectra. Fig. S1 shows the XRD patterns of the CDs with different concentrations. One broad peak was observed around 2θ = 20°, indicating the amorphous phase of CDs as reported in the literature [21].

To understand the size and morphology of all the CDs samples under optimal synthesis conditions were characterized by TEM and HRTEM. In Fig. 2a, the TEM images and their corresponding histograms showed that the narrow size distribution with similar statistical diameters of 3.15 nm (50 random nanoparticles were analyzed), regardless of CDs concentration. The TEM (Fig. 2a) and HRTEM images (Fig. 2b) clearly confirmed that as-prepared CDs exhibit a relatively uniform and well-dispersed spherical shape with lattice spacing of about 0.32 nm corresponding to the 002 facet of graphite.

The color of the CDs samples gradually changed from reddish brown to light yellow (in web version) with decreasing the concentration of CDs under the visible light (Fig. 3 b). The CDs exhibited clear multiple colors under excitation wavelength light, as shown in Fig. 3b. The optical properties of CDs were investigated by UV–vis absorption and PL spectroscopy at room temperature. Fig. 3a shows the absorption spectra of CDs with different concentrations. All samples showed the characteristic absorption peak centered at 260 nm (≈4.77 eV) and 359 nm (≈3.45 eV), which can be assigned to the π–π* transition of the CC bonds and the n-π* transition of the CO bonds, respectively. The absorption spectrum is similar to that of the CDs reported previously in the literature [22,23]. As shown in Fig. 3a, the absorption peak positions remained unchanged for all samples with different concentrations.

The PL properties of the as-prepared CDs were investigated. Fig. 4 shows the emission spectra of the CDs with different concentrations under several excitation wavelengths from 360 to 480 nm. All types of CDs herein emit at fluorescence wavelengths independent of excitation wavelengths, whereas the PL intensity remarkably increased with the increase of excitation wavelength (Fig. 4a–e). Moreover, it is noticed that the fluorescence emission peaks of prepared CDs samples were clearly blue-shifts from 620 nm, 605 nm, 575 nm, 550 nm–530 nm when the concentration of CDs decreased (Fig. 4f). What are responsible for the tunable emission? Surface states influenced by surface modification can serve as capturing center of excitations, thereby giving rise to the surface-state-related fluorescence [24,25]. Some study about the concentration dependent emission of CDs have also been reported [11]. The silane-prefunctionalised CDs contain many alkyloxysilyl groups, similar to the silane coupling agent. After hydrolysis, many organic compounds will form silano groups which are highly reactive intermediates in bonding and the self- or hybrid-condensation with other silanols and hydroxyl groups [21]. The highly reactive intermediates on the surface of the CDs will affect the surface states influenced by the surface modification of the CDs. After adding the ethanol, it will have a higher degree of hydrolysis and condensation among the varieties of reactive intermediates on the surface of the CDs. Through hydrolysis and condensation, transferred reactive intermediates such as silano and alkoxysilyl groups into stable Si–O–Si bonds, which can transfer CDs from defect-state emission into intrinsic-state emission. In this process, the silano and multialkoxysilyl groups changed into Si–O–Si bonds, which suppressed the nonradiative process and further enhanced the integrity of the π-conjugated system (it also reduced the defects). With the increase of amounts of ethanol, surface interactions such as hydrolysis and condensation among the varieties of reactive intermediates on the surface of the CDs become obvious [21,26], which was consistent with the FTIR results. Such a change consequently broaden the energy levels and causes the blue-shift fluorescence emission of the CDs [12]. Therefore, the PL peaks of the resulting CDs moved to shorter wavelengths as the hydrolysis reaction between among the varieties of reactive intermediates increases. This behavior is consistent with previous studies [27], suggesting surface-state-related fluorescence from CDs. The PL thermal stability performance of CDs was also measured. Fig. S2 depicts the temperature-dependent emission of CDs. And the emission intensity clearly depended on the temperature, the decrease in emission intensity with increasing temperature.

To get further insight into the mechanism of this effect, the average lifetime of all the CDs samples were determined using the TRPL technique. The TRPL spectra of the CDs with different concentrations were shown in Fig. 5 under the excitation of 400 nm. The PL decay curves are fitted by a double exponential function:
(1)
Y
( t )
=
α 1
exp
(
− τ
/
t 1
)
+
α 2
exp
(
− τ
/
t 2
)
where α1 and α2 are the fractional contributions of decay lifetime τ1 and τ2.

The average lifetime can be calculated by a relation:
(2)
τ
a v
=
(
α 1
τ 1 2
+
α 2
τ 2 2
)
/
(
α 1
τ 1
+
α 2
τ 2
)

The emission decays of all the CDs are composed of two processes: a short-lived component τ1 (1–4 ns) and a long-lived component τ2 (7–10 ns), which are related to the electronic transitions in the CDs, indicating the strong coupling between the core and surface sates similar to the luminescent Si nanoparticles [28]. The fitting parameters were summarized in Table 1 , the average lifetimes of CDs are clearly dependent of the doping concentrations of CDs which were evidently increased when the doping concentration of CDs increased. Next, we estimated the radiative and non-radiative decay rates of CDs with different concentrations as listed in Table 2 from Eqs. (3) and (4):
(3)
Q Y =
k r
k r
+
k
n r
(4)
1 τ
=
k r
+
k
n r
Where kr and knr are the radiative and non-radiative decay rates, τ is the average lifetime. As seen from Table 1, the average lifetimes of CDs were clearly dependent of the concentrations which were evidently decreased when the concentration of CDs decreased. Moreover, the fluorescence knr was calculated to be on the order of 108 s−1. While the evident surface interactions of CDs among reactive intermediate, resulting in the weaken of knr from 0.47 × 108 s−1 for the CDs with the concentration of C to 0.05 × 108 s−1 for the concentration of 1/5C, meaning that the surfaces interactions among reactive intermediates such as silano and alkoxysilyl groups, which suppresses non-radiative decay processes. These recombination processes are related to the electronic transitions in the CDs, indicating the strong coupling between the core and surface sates [29]. Thus we speculated that the change in the extent of the π-electron system due to surface hydrolysis and condensation among varieties of reactive intermediates affect the energy levels of the surface electronic states. The higher degree of chemical reaction among reactive intermediates on the CDs surface will result in an increase in the extent of the π-electron system. And this will lower the energy gap of the π–π* transitions and further result in a small energy gap for the surface states [30].Such a change consequently causes the blue-shift fluorescence emission of the CDs. So high concentration CDs possessed long-wavelength fluorescence. Based on these observations, we asserted that the PL of the CDs are of surface-state emission and the energy gap of the surface emissive sites is a function of both the π-electron system and surface chemistry.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Third-order non-linear optical properties of CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The third-order non-linear optical (NLO) properties of CDs with different concentrations have been investigated by Z-scan technique. In order to determine the nonlinear refraction and absorption of the CDs, open aperture (OA) and closed aperture (CA) Z-scan measurements were obtained under 532 nm, 190 fs laser excitation conditions. Figs. 6–7
represented CA and OA Z-scan results with single-pulse energy of 0.07 μJ for all CDs sample and the ethanol, respectively. Here the black squares in the Fig. 6 indicated the experimental result while solid line for theoretical fit of the normalized transmittance. Using similar measurements performed on different concentration of CDs the nonlinear absorption coefficient (β) and the nonlinear refractive parameter (γ), both these were calculated from the OA and CA Z-scan curves according to the well-known theory [31].

The real and imaginary parts of the third-order nonlinear susceptibility (χ(3)) were deduced according to the parameters β and γ, as shown in Eqs. (5) and (6) [32].
(5)
Im
χ
( 3 )
(
e s u
)
=
λ
ε 0
c 2
n 0 2
4
π 2
β
(
m / W
)
(6)
Re
χ
( 3 )
(
e s u
)
=
ε 0
c 2
n 0 2
π
γ
(
m 2
/ W
)
Where λ, ε0, c and n0 are the wavelength of the laser light, permittivity of free space, speed of light and linear refractive index of the glass, respectively. The total optical third-order nonlinear susceptibility χ(3) is given by Eq. (7) [33].
(7)
|
χ
( 3 )
|
(
e s u
)
=
{
(
Re
χ
( 3 )
)
2
+
(
I m
χ
( 3 )
)
2
}
1 2

All of the nonlinear parameters were listed in Table 2. Fig. 6 showed CA Z-scan results for CDs sample and the ethanol exhibited a pre-focal transmission valley followed by a post-focal transmission peak, indicative of self-focusing effect. However, since the variation of the transmission (ΔTp–v) of all the CDs sample were found to be higher than that of the ethanol, it can be concluded that as-prepared silane pre-functionalized CDs possess positive sign γ. Moreover, the nonlinear refractive parameter γ of CDs were clearly dependent of the dilute concentrations which were evidently decreased when the concentration of CDs solution of CDs decreased. On the other hand, as depicted from the practically flat OA Z-scan in Fig. 7, the CDs solutions were found to exhibit significant nonlinear saturable absorption (RA) while the ethanol did not have any NLO response. Additionally, the nonlinear absorption coefficient β of CDs decreased with decreasing the concentration, while the concentration of CDs reached 1/20C, it did not have NLO response.

In addition, for most of third-order nonlinear-based optical devices, such as all-optical switching, super continuum generation, nonlinear refraction is a beneficial parameter [34], therefore, Figure of merit (F = γ/βλ) was defined to evaluate the third-order nonlinear performance of materials at a certain wavelength [35]. For the CDs with different concentrations, as shown in Table 2, the maximum F value exceeding 2.73. Moreover, the F values of CDs were clearly dependent of the dilute concentrations which were evidently increased when the concentration of CDs solution of CDs decreased. And the F value increased near 47% after decrease the concentrations of CDs, indicating improvement of the third-order nonlinear performance of CDs after the dilution of CDs.It has reported that the surface modification or doping could affect the optical properties of CDs, mainly because of surface modification and doping was employed in semiconductors in order to modify their electronic properties [36], and it was associated with the formation of new electronic states. Such new electronic states can alter the NLO optical properties as well as PL properties.

In summary, a facile method for synthesizing highly luminescent CDs with tunable excitation-independent fluorescence emission wavelengths by simple adjustment of the concentrations has been developed. The prepared silane pre-functionalized CDs solution was diluted by ethanol providing stable, optically transparent CDs dispersion with different colors. Significantly, the tunable fluorescence of emission peaks clearly depended on the concentration of CDs (from C to 1/20C). This resulted in blue-shifted excitation-independent fluorescence emission wavelengths from 620 to 530 nm with remarkably enhanced emission intensity observed when concentration of CD decreased. Moreover, the third-order nonlinear optical responses of CDs exhibited positive nonlinear refractivity and negative nonlinear absorption coefficient. Both the nonlinear refractivity and negative nonlinear absorption coefficient are decreased when the concentration of CDs decreased. And the F value increased near 47% after decrease the concentrations of CDs, indicating improvement of the third-order nonlinear performance of CDs after the dilution of CDs. These tunable optical properties would offer more possible applications for the utility of CDs in optical devices.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was financially supported by the Zhejiang Province Natural Science Foundation (LQ14E020006) and National Nature Science Foundation of China (51272059, 51472183 and 51172165).

ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2016.01.005.
